coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive optical fiber SPR sensor enhanced by Au-NPs-film modified with functionalized CQDs for label-free detecting cobalt (II) ion
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Jianguo
Xia, Binyun
Niu, Kunpeng
Wang, Jiale
Li, Jinhua
Wang, Ning
coredata.dc:description:
Background Cobalt, an essential trace element, is vital for maintaining human nervous system function, aiding in DNA synthesis, and contributing to red blood cell production. It is helpful for disease diagnosis and treatment plan evaluation by precisely monitoring its concentration changes in the human body. Despite extensive efforts made, due to its ultra-low concentration, the current limit of detection (LOD) as reported is still inadequate and cannot be satisfied with the precise clinical applications. Therefore, it is crucial to develop novel label-free sensors with high sensitivity and excellent selectivity for detecting trace amounts of Co2+.
Results Here, an ultrasensitive optical fiber SPR sensor was designed and fabricated for label-free detection of Co2+ with ultra-low concentration. It is achieved by modifying the carboxyl-functionalized CQDs on the AuNPs/Au film-coated hetero-core fiber, which can specifically capture the Co2+, leading to changes in the fiber's surface refractive index (RI) and subsequent SPR wavelength shifts in the transmission spectrum. Both the Au film and AuNPs on the fiber are modified with CQDs, leveraging their large surface area to enhance the number of active sites and probes. The sensor exhibits an ultra-high sensitivity of approximately 6.67 × 1019 nm/M, and the LOD is obtained as low as 5.36 × 10−20 M which is several orders of magnitude lower compared to other conventional methods. It is also experimentally demonstrated that the sensor possesses excellent specificity, stability, and repeatability, which may be adapted for detecting real clinical samples.
Significance The CQDs-functionalized optical fiber SPR sensor exhibits substantial potential for precisely detecting Co2+ of trace amounts, which is especially vital for scarce clinical samples. Additionally, the sensing platform with sample sensor fabrication and measurement configuration introduces a novel, highly sensitive approach to biochemical analysis, particularly adapting for applications involving the detection of trace targets, which could also be employed to detect various biochemical targets by facile modification of CQDs with specific groups or biomolecules.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cobalt (II) ions
Surface plasmon resonance
Carbon quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles
MSM fiber structure
Optical fiber sensor)

The effect of AuNPs on the sensor performance
Selectivity, repeatability, and stability of the sensors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The ultrasensitive optical fiber SPR sensor is modified with AuNPs/CQDs. (a) The 3D schematic diagram and working principle of the SPR sensor for the accurate measurement of Co2+. (b) The experimental setup of the SPR sensor for the accurate measurement of Co2+.
Schematic illustration of the fabrication processes of the optical fiber SPR sensor modified with functionalized CQDs.

(a) TEM images of CQDs (the inset is their High-resolution TEM images). (b) FTIR spectrum of CQDs. (c) UV–visible absorption spectra of the solution samples (CQDs, AuNPs, CQDs/AuNPs, and CQDs/AuNPs/Co2+). (d) SEM images of AuNPs modified on the Au film-coated SPR fiber. (e) Transmission spectra of AuNPs, CQDs, and Co2+ modified onto the Au film-coated SPR fiber. (f) XPS spectra of optical fiber SPR sensor functionalized by AuNPs/CQDs before and after the addition of Co2+.

Detection of Co2+ using the SPR sensors: (a) the transmission spectra of the optical fiber SPR sensor modified with AuNPs/CQDs; (b) the calibration curve of wavelength shift versus Co2+ concentration. (c) the wavelength shift (Δλ) and detection time of the optical fiber SPR sensor with different modification materials; (d) the selectivity of the optical fiber SPR sensor modified with AuNPs/CQDs; (e) the repeatability of the optical fiber SPR sensor modified with AuNPs/CQDs; (f) the stability of the optical fiber SPR sensor modified with AuNPs/CQDs.
Comparison of different methods for the detection of Co2+.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive optical fiber SPR sensor enhanced by Au-NPs-film modified with functionalized CQDs for label-free detecting cobalt (II) ion

Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials Co-Constructed by the Province and Ministry
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials Co-Constructed by the Province and Ministry, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Ministry of Education, Wuhan 430062, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cobalt, an essential trace element, is vital for maintaining human nervous system function, aiding in DNA synthesis, and contributing to red blood cell production. It is helpful for disease diagnosis and treatment plan evaluation by precisely monitoring its concentration changes in the human body. Despite extensive efforts made, due to its ultra-low concentration, the current limit of detection (LOD) as reported is still inadequate and cannot be satisfied with the precise clinical applications. Therefore, it is crucial to develop novel label-free sensors with high sensitivity and excellent selectivity for detecting trace amounts of Co2+.

Here, an ultrasensitive optical fiber SPR sensor was designed and fabricated for label-free detection of Co2+ with ultra-low concentration. It is achieved by modifying the carboxyl-functionalized CQDs on the AuNPs/Au film-coated hetero-core fiber, which can specifically capture the Co2+, leading to changes in the fiber's surface refractive index (RI) and subsequent SPR wavelength shifts in the transmission spectrum. Both the Au film and AuNPs on the fiber are modified with CQDs, leveraging their large surface area to enhance the number of active sites and probes. The sensor exhibits an ultra-high sensitivity of approximately 6.67 × 1019 nm/M, and the LOD is obtained as low as 5.36 × 10−20 M which is several orders of magnitude lower compared to other conventional methods. It is also experimentally demonstrated that the sensor possesses excellent specificity, stability, and repeatability, which may be adapted for detecting real clinical samples.

The CQDs-functionalized optical fiber SPR sensor exhibits substantial potential for precisely detecting Co2+ of trace amounts, which is especially vital for scarce clinical samples. Additionally, the sensing platform with sample sensor fabrication and measurement configuration introduces a novel, highly sensitive approach to biochemical analysis, particularly adapting for applications involving the detection of trace targets, which could also be employed to detect various biochemical targets by facile modification of CQDs with specific groups or biomolecules.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cobalt is an essential trace element that is crucial for maintaining the normal bio function of the nervous, facilitating DNA synthesis, and aiding in the production of red blood cells [1,2]. It is primarily found in the liver, kidneys, and bones in the form of ions with trace amounts of approximately 1.1–1.5 mg [3]. A cobalt deficiency can result in diseases like cardiovascular sclerosis, neurodegeneration, and asthma [4–6], while prolonged exposure to or acute ingestion of excess cobalt may cause congestive heart disease, neurological damage, and pancreatic damage [7–9]. It is particularly concerning that the common use of cobalt in radiation therapy for malignant tumors and cancers may cause cobalt poisoning or inflammation [10,11]. Therefore, it is imperative to develop non-biotoxic, highly sensitive, and selective sensors for monitoring trace Co2+, holding great significance in clinical medicine and beyond.

Typical methods for Co2+ detection include colorimetry [12], fluorescence spectrometry [13], atomic absorption spectrometry [14], and electrochemistry [15]. Colorimetric methods are user-friendly and cost-effective, albeit with low sensitivity. Fluorescence spectrometry, atomic absorption spectrometry, and electrochemistry offer high sensitivity and selectivity, normally involving complex sample labeling. Beyond those, optical fiber sensors are widely recognized as a cost-effective and convenient tool for detecting biochemical targets by merits of their high sensitivity, label-free nature, resistance to electromagnetic interference, ease of fabrication, and low cost. Yu et al. reported absorption detection of Co2+ ions in aqueous solutions using a solid-core photonic crystal fiber (PCF) [16], but it had no selectivity. Kang et al. reported a micro-tapered long-period fiber grating (MTLPFG) modified by polydopamine-functionalized graphene oxide for Co2+ detection, which was demonstrated with the limit of detection (LOD) of 1 ppb [17]. Nevertheless, the above-mentioned optical fiber Co2+ sensors are prone to interference from other ions in the environment, and they involve complex fabrication, leading to low sensitivity and high cost. To address these challenges, plasmonic nanoparticles (NPs)-mediated surface plasmon resonance (SPR) sensors emerge as an optimal solution [18,19]. Over the past decades, optical fiber SPR sensors [20–22] have been extensively utilized in biochemical sensing for the rapid, label-free detection of diverse biomolecules and chemicals, including heavy metal ions, leveraging their high sensitivity and real-time monitoring capabilities [23–25]. Antohe reported a polyaniline/platinum (PANI/Pt) bilayer optical fiber sensor utilizing the FO-SPR technique for the detection of Co2+ in aqueous solutions. It exhibited a sensitivity of 37.62 nm/μM and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.14 μM [26].

Although significant efforts have been made, the LOD of the aforementioned sensors remains insufficient for practical application in biochemical testing and clinical medical monitoring, owing to the very low concentration of cobalt in the human body. To enhance the performance of optical fiber Co2+ sensors and reduce their LOD, it is necessary to improve both fiber structure and sensitive materials. Iga et al. proposed a novel multimode-singlemode-multimode fiber (MMF-SMF-MMF) hetero-core structure for refractive index (RI) measurement. A silver-coated SMF serves as a sensing probe, utilizing SPR effects to achieve high sensitivity and mitigate complex fabrication and inferior mechanical robustness [27,28]. These sensors exhibit excellent biocompatibility, high sensitivity, and stability. Moreover, the selection of sensitive materials must be carefully considered to specifically trap Co2+, such as their immobilization on fibers, morphology, and optical properties. Current sensitive materials for Co2+ detection commonly include metal-organic framework materials (MOFs), inorganic materials, and some nanomaterials. MOFs, like Fe2O3@MIL-53(Al), offer benefits such as a large specific surface area, strong adsorption of heavy metal ions, outstanding chemical and thermal stability, and recyclability. However, they are prone to interference from other water ions and lack selectivity for different heavy metals [29]. Inorganic materials, particularly metal oxides, provide stability and resistance to interference, making them suitable for detection under various environments, but their preparation is intricate and expensive with limited ion selectivity [30]. Some nanomaterials such as MoS2 nanosheets and graphene are popular research subjects due to their high specific surface areas and detection sensitivities, making them ideal for detecting low-concentration heavy metal ions. Nonetheless, the application of certain nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes in the detection of heavy metal ions suffers from some drawbacks, including difficulty in creating a uniform film, issues with functionalization, stability concerns, low selectivity, poor reproducibility, and high production costs [31]. In contrast, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as representative zero-dimensional nanomaterials, are extensively used in biochemical sensing due to their advantageous properties, such as high quantum yield, excellent chemical stability, and biocompatibility [32,33]. However, a lot of research work on CQDs-based sensors mainly utilizes its fluorescence characteristics, yet they suffer from stability concerns, inadequate biocompatibility, and selectivity issues [34]. Additionally, CQDs can be readily modified with functional groups or biomolecules to selectively recognize various detection targets, including heavy metal ions and DNA [35,36]. By leveraging the high RI sensitivity of SPR fibers, diverse sensors with high sensitivity and low LOD can be developed through tailored functionalization for specific targets.

This study developed an ultrasensitive, label-free optical fiber SPR sensor for the detection of trace amounts of Co2+. The sensor is primarily composed of an AuNPs/Au film-coated MMF-SMF-MMF hetero-core fiber and carboxyl-functionalized CQDs, which act as probes for specifically capturing the Co2+. The sensor harnesses the synergistic enhancement effect of AuNPs-modified Au films on the fiber and the extensive surface area of CQDs to achieve an ultra-low LOD of approximately 5.36 × 10−20 M for Co2+, significantly lower than the limits achieved by conventional detection methods. Additionally, the sensor offers a broad detection range with high selectivity and stability. This optical fiber SPR sensing platform is versatile for detecting trace amounts of various biochemical substances by modifying the probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and working principle of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 a shows a 3D schematic of the SPR sensor probe and its mechanism for Co2+ detection. The sensor features a hetero-core structure of MMF-SMF-MMF. It comprises an SMF segment, approximately 15 mm in length, fused at each end with MMF. An Au film with approximately 60 nm in thickness, covers the SMF segment. Furthermore, AuNPs with a diameter of around 22.65 nm are modified and immobilized on the Au film surface, and carboxyl-functionalized CQDs are bonded to the gold substrates (including both Au films and AuNPs) via chemical modification techniques.

The proposed sensor leverages the SPR synergistic enhancement effect of Au films and AuNPs for highly sensitive Co2+ detection and utilizes functionalized CQDs to capture Co2+. The sensor probe's working principle is depicted in Fig. 1a. The incident light from the broadband light source is transmitted from MMF to SMF, and some light is coupled into the SMF's cladding, propagating in cladding modes [37]. When the incident light's wavelength aligns with the Au film/AuNPs' resonance wavelength, photons interact with the electron surface, generating surface plasmon waves or localized surface plasmon waves [38,39]. The incident light's energy interacts with the charges on the Au film/AuNPs, partially transferring energy to the Au film/AuNPs' surface. Consequently, the sensor's transmission spectrum exhibits absorption dips at the resonance wavelength, which are highly sensitive to changes in the surrounding medium's RI. Changes in Co2+ solution concentrations cause interactions with the CQDs on the Au film/AuNPs, altering the interface's RI and leading to a shift in the resonance wavelength [40]. It enables the determination of changes in Co2+ concentration in the solution by recording the transmitted light's wavelength shift. The response of the optical fiber SPR sensor can be described by the following equation:
(1)
Δ λ
max
= m Δ n
{
1 − exp
(
− 2 d
l d
)
}
(2)
Δ I
max
= m Δ n
{
1 − exp
(
− 2 d
l d
)
}
where
Δ λ
max
is the variations in wavelength shift;
Δ I
max
is the variation in light intensity;
m
is the sensitivity of the RI;
Δ n
is the change in RI;
d
is the thickness of the effective adsorbed layer; and
l d
is the decay length of the surface plasma wave.

The sensing platform's experimental setup, depicted in Fig. 1b, primarily comprises a tungsten-halogen lamp, an optical displacement platform, an optical fiber spectrometer, a computer, and a V-groove. The RI sensitivity of the MMF-SMF-MMF hetero-core optical fiber probe was assessed by monitoring the SPR-induced wavelength shifts in its transmission spectra. The optical fiber probe was thoroughly cleaned with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) before each measurement.

DL-Dithiothreitol (DDT, C4H10O2S2, 99 %), mercaptoacetic acid (MAA， C2H4O2S, 98 %), N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC， C8H17N3∙HCl, 98 %), N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS, C4H5NO3, 98 %), dl-thioctic acid (C8H14O2S2, 99 %), triethylenetetramine (C6H18N4, 97 %), albumin, hemoglobin, the chlorine salts (Co2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Na+, K+) and nitrate salts (Cu2+, Mg2+, Zn2+) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Chloroauric acid tetrahydrate (HAuCl4·4H2O, 99.99 %) and trisodium citrate dihydrate were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All samples were prepared fresh prior to testing in order to avoid experimental errors. All the chemicals were of analytical grade without further purification. All solutions were prepared using deionized (DI) water.

The single-mode fibers (SMFs) (core/cladding diameter, 8/125 μm) and the multi-mode fibers (MMFs) (core/cladding diameter, 62.5/125 μm) were purchased from Yangtze Optical Fiber and Cable Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China).

The hetero-core structure optical fiber (MMF-SMF-MMF) was fabricated by using the fusion splicer (FSM-60s, Fujikura Ltd., Japan). The Au film was deposited onto the SMF section by magnetron sputtering (Bestec GmbH, Berlin, Germany). The absorption spectra of the sample solutions were measured by the UV–visible spectrophotometer (AvaSpec-ULS2048L, Avantes Co. Ltd., Beijing, China) to characterize the synthesized AuNPs and CQDs. The morphology and crystallization of the synthesized CQDs were investigated by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM2100F, JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan), while the AuNPs on the SMF were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Zeiss G300, Carl Zeiss AG, Oberkochen, Baden-Wurttemberg, Germany). The surface of the sensor probes was also observed by the optical microscope (LEICA S9i, Leica Microsystems Co., Ltd., Wetzlar, Germany). Finally, the optical sensing characteristics of the sensors were explored by configuring a sensing platform composed of a tungsten-halogen lamp light source (HL-2000, Ocean Optics Inc., USA), an optical fiber spectrometer (Flame-T-VIS-NIR, Ocean Optics, USA), a V-groove, and an optical displacement platform.

First, the MMF-SMF-MMF hetero-core optical fiber was fabricated, and the manufacturing processes are illustrated in Fig. S1 of the Supplementary Material (SM). Subsequently, a 60-nm-thick Au film was coated on the SMF section to produce an SPR fiber. After that, the AuNPs with a diameter of approximately 22 nm were synthesized using the Frens method [41] and dispersed in an aqueous solution. The AuNPs preparation is shown in detail in Fig. S2 of SM. The CQDs were also prepared via the classical hydrothermal method [42], and the preparation processes are detailed in Fig. S3 of SM.

Afterward, the AuNPs and carboxyl-functionalized CQDs were successively modified onto the Au film-coated MSM fiber, with the specific procedures detailed in Fig. 2 . Initially, a 0.5 M DTT solution was prepared by dissolving 0.0771 g of DDT in 1 mL of DI water. Subsequently, the Au-film coated SMF segment was immersed in the DTT solution for 1.5 h, followed by being soaked with AuNPs solution for 3 h. Thereafter, the optical fiber was removed and dried in air, facilitating adherence of AuNPs to the Au film via Au–S bonds. Subsequently, the Au-film-coated SMF segment was fully immersed in the 50 mM MAA solution in the dark overnight. A mixed solution of EDC (0.2 mM) and NHS (0.5 mM) in PBS (pH 5.5) was prepared to activate the carboxyl groups on the Au-film-coated SMF segment for 2 h. EDC, an activator for carboxylic acids, reacts with the carboxyl groups on MAA to produce an activated ester intermediate [43]. During this process, EDC converts the carboxyl groups into a reactive O-acylisourea intermediate, while NHS, with its hydroxyl group, reacts with the activated ester to form a stable active ester [44]. This active ester then reacts with the amino groups on CQDs to form amide bonds, facilitating the coupling of carboxyl and amino groups, and the chemical linkage between MAA and CQDs [45,46], as illustrated in Fig. S4. Subsequently, the SMF segment was immersed in the 0.5 mg/mL CQDs solution for 3 h, and a 3D schematic of the immobilization process between AuNPs and CQDs is provided in Fig. S5 of SM. Lastly, the Au-coated SMF segment was rinsed by using the PBS buffer three times to eliminate any remaining unfixed CQDs and other contaminants. The mechanisms of surface material loading and functionalization during sensor fabrication are depicted in Fig. S6.

To detect Co2+, standard buffered Co2+ solutions with concentrations ranging from 10−20 to 10−4 M were prepared in DI water for concentration measurements. The optical fiber sensor was submerged in 750 μL of Co2+ solution at various concentrations for 20 min and thoroughly rinsed with PBS after each measurement. All experimental tests were conducted at a temperature of 23 ± 0.1 °C. The selectivity of the sensor was investigated using 13 typical metal ions at a concentration of 1 × 10−8 M as interferents. The sensor probe was subjected to six replicate measurements using Co2+ solutions at concentrations of 10−20, 10−16, 10−12, 10−8, and 10−4 M, respectively. The sensor probe was soaked in Co2+ solutions of concentrations 10−4, 10−12, and 10−20 M for 16 h under consistent experimental conditions to assess its stability. Additionally, the response time of the sensor and the real response characteristics of a simulated sample with serum as solvent and Co2+ as solute were measured.

Fig. 3 a presents TEM images of CQDs, displaying their uniform distribution as spherical nanoparticles without aggregation. The average diameter of the CQDs is approximately 7.61 nm (see Fig. S7 for details). Fig. 3a, HRTEM image shows that the synthesized CQDs exhibit robust crystallinity with a lattice spacing of around 0.27 nm, corresponding to the graphite (100) plane. This suggests an intact single-crystal structure for the CQDs. The FTIR spectrum of the CQDs (Fig. 3b) reveals absorption bands at 2972, 2885, 1655, and 1452 cm−1, corresponding to O–H, N–H, C=O, and C=C stretching vibrations, respectively. Peaks at 1381 cm−1, 1327 cm−1, and 1273 cm−1 are assigned to C–N, C–O, and C–S bonds, respectively. The data indicate that nitrogen and sulfur are effectively doped into the CQDs, and their surfaces are rich in amino and carboxyl groups. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is used to analyze the chemical composition of the CQDs. The XPS spectrum of the CQDs (Fig. S8) reveals four peaks at 285.13, 398.84, 164.00, and 530.84 eV, corresponding to C 1s (46.35 %), N 1s (14.28 %), S 2p (6.41 %), and O 1s (32.96 %) peaks, respectively. The presence of the N 1s signal further confirms the functionalization of CQDs with a nitrogen-containing group. Additionally, these findings corroborate the doping of N and S in the CQDs and the presence of extensive carboxyl and amino groups on their surfaces.

Fig. 3c presents the absorption spectra of CQDs, AuNPs, CQDs-AuNPs, and the CQDs-AuNP-Co2+ complex. CQDs display a distinct absorption at 325 nm, suggesting robust chemical bonding, whereas AuNPs feature an obvious absorption at 520 nm, which indicates that the AuNPs disperse in aqueous with a diameter of about 22 nm [41], as verified by Fig. 3d. The absorbance peaks for the AuNPs-CQD are at 326 nm and 620 nm, indicating CQD immobilized successfully on AuNPs via chemical bonding. After adding the Co2+, the AuNPs-CQD absorbance peaks diminish, verifying Co2+ binding to AuNPs-CQDs, mediated by electrostatic attractions between the Co2+ cations and the carboxylate groups of AuNPs-CQDs [47]. Fig. 3d features the SEM image of AuNPs deposited on an Au film-coated SPR fiber. Fig. S9 reveals that AuNPs have an average size of ∼22.65 nm. Furthermore, AuNPs increased the Au film's surface area by a factor of 3.15 upon modification through approximate calculation, which is expected to improve the sensitivity of optical fiber sensors for Co2+ detection. Fig. 3e exhibits the transmission spectra upon modification of AuNPs, CQDs, and Co2+ on the Au film-coated SPR fiber. The transmission spectra of SPR fiber optic sensors coated with Au film were red-shifted after the addition of AuNPs, CQDs, and Co2+. Fig. 3f shows the XPS spectra of the optical fiber SPR sensor modified with AuNPs/CQDs before and after the addition of Co2+ in PBS solution. The appearance of a Co 3s peak suggests successful Co2+ binding to the functionalized CQDs. Consequently, the functionalized CQDs on the sensor can effectively capture and detect Co2+ in solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of AuNPs on the sensor performance
ce:section.ce:para: Experimental studies were conducted on SPR fibers modified with AuNPs and unmodified AuNPs to confirm the enhancement of the SPR effect on the fiber surface. The results are presented in Fig. S10.

To evaluate the SPR sensors' detection capabilities for Co2+, we used both AuNP-modified and unmodified sensors to measure Co2+ solutions at varying concentrations, maintaining consistency in all other experimental parameters. The transmission spectrum's wavelength shift indicates the sensor's response to Co2+. Fig. 4 a illustrates the response of the AuNPs/CQD-modified optical fiber SPR sensor to Co2+. At the Co2+ concentration of 10−4 M, the average wavelength shift in the transmission spectrum was about 27.7 nm. Even at the low Co2+ concentration of 10−20 M, the average wavelength shift in the transmission spectrum was 2.61 nm. According to Eq. (3), the sensor's LOD can be calculated and yields 5.36 × 10−20 M.
(3)
L O D =
3 σ
s
Where LOD is the limit of detection, σ is the standard deviation of blank probe sample measurements, and s is the slope of the calibration plot.

To ensure that the wavelength shift comes from detecting Co2+, especially at ultra-low concentrations, the control experiments were also conducted. The MSM-structured optical fiber SPR sensor was immersed in a Co2+-free control sample (0 mol/L) for 30 min under consistent experimental conditions to assess the measurement background noise. Fig. S11 shows the wavelength shift of the optical fiber sensor in the absence of Co2+. It can be observed with a maximum resonance wavelength shift of 0.29 nm over the 30 min measurement. This result indicates that background noise is negligible relative to the target signal, ensuring minimal impact on measurement accuracy. Additionally, the sensor exhibits a linear detection range from 1 × 10−20 M to 1 × 10−4 M, which might benefit from the CQDs' large specific surface area and the quantum size effect. These attributes enable CQDs to adsorb cobalt ions effectively. Furthermore, CQDs can adsorb a broader range of light wavelengths than other nanomaterials. This property enhances the sensor's response to optical signals at various wavelengths, significantly improving its sensitivity for Co2+ detection. On the other hand, the optical fiber sensor coated with pure Au film modified with AuNPs showed improved sensitivity due to the synergistic enhancement effect of SPR and LSPR. The CQDs-modified optical fiber SPR sensor without AuNP is also experimentally demonstrated with an LOD of 4.26 × 10−12 M ranging from 1 × 10−12 to 1 × 10−4 M, as shown in Fig. S12. The effective wavelength shift for each optical fiber sensor was determined by analyzing the transmission spectrum's wavelength shift at varying Co2+ concentrations, as depicted in Fig. 4b. The sensor's effective wavelength shift was found to rise with increasing Co2+ concentration, demonstrating an excellent linear correlation between the wavelength shift and Co2+ concentration within 10−20 M to 10−4 M.

Fig. 4c compares the wavelength shift and response time of SPR sensors modified with CQDs and AuNPs/CQDs when detecting Co2+ at the concentration of 10−12 M. The sensor modified with AuNPs/CQDs exhibits a greater maximum wavelength shift (from 7.88 nm to 16.19 nm) and a shorter detection time (from 30 min to 20 min) compared to the sensor modified with CQDs. The improved sensor performance is primarily attributed to AuNPs acting as sensitizers for the CQDs coated on the Au film-coated optical fiber.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, repeatability, and stability of the sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is crucial for optical fiber sensors, particularly in quantifying metal ions in complex environments. Typical ions including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Cl−, HCO3 − and SO4 2−, were investigated as potential interfering species under the same experimental conditions as referred in section 4.3. Interfering ions were prepared at a concentration of 1 × 10−8 M, and the pH was adjusted to approximately 4.0. Fig. 4d illustrates that the SPR sensor exhibited the strongest response to Co2+, characterized by a resonance wavelength shift of 21.49 nm. Additionally, the sensor demonstrated the largest resonance wavelength shift of about 2.8 nm for Cu2+ among the interfering metal ions. However, the resonance wavelength shift for Co2+ was 7.65 times greater than that for Cu2+. Additional biological simulants, such as albumin and hemoglobin, were also detected under the same experimental conditions as previously described. Biological simulants at a concentration of 1 × 10−8 M were prepared, and the sensor exhibited resonance wavelength shifts of 2.6 nm for albumin and 4.6 nm for hemoglobin. Meanwhile, to verify the high selectivity of the sensors, the sensors were also measured under conditions of different concentrations of interferents. Interfering ions were prepared at concentrations of 1 × 10−4 mol/L, 1 × 10−12 mol/L, 1 × 10−16 mol/L, and 1 × 10−20 mol/L, while maintaining the ambient pH at approximately 4.0. Fig. S13 illustrates the sensor's strong response to Co2+, showing wavelength shifts of 27.51 nm, 16.11 nm, 8.43 nm, 2.43 nm, and 2.54 nm at Co2+ concentrations of 1 × 10−4 mol/L, 1 × 10−12 mol/L, 1 × 10−16 mol/L, and 1 × 10−20 mol/L, respectively.

The ultra-sensitive optical fiber SPR sensor modified with AuNPs/CQDs demonstrates superior selectivity for Co2+ detection compared to other interferents. This can be attributed to the coordination reaction between functionalized CQDs and Co2+, resulting in excellent binding properties between Co2+ and functionalized CQDs. Therefore, the ultra-sensitive optical fiber SPR sensor functionalized with AuNPs/CQDs shows higher selectivity in detecting Co2+ compared to other interferents. This change in selectivity may involve resonance energy transfer (RET) between the CQDs and Co2+ [48]. As shown in Figs. S14 and S15, the UV–visible absorption and fluorescence intensities of the CQDs are at their lowest values in the presence of Co2+ compared to other interferents. This could be because the d-orbital electronic energy levels of the Co2+ are relatively matched to the π*-orbital electronic energy levels of the CQDs, allowing the fluorescence emission energy of the CQDs to be transferred to the Co2+ through non-radiative means [49,50], leading to decreased fluorescence intensity and attenuated UV–Visible absorption intensity of the CQDs. Moreover, the d-orbital electronic energy levels of the Co2+ and the π*-orbital electronic energy levels of the CQDs may have better spatial overlap, which is also conducive to the occurrence of energy transfer [51,52].

Repeatability is also very important for assessing the sensor performance. We evaluated the sensor by immersing it into the Co2+ solutions with concentrations ranging from 10−20 to 10−4 M. Following each measurement, the sensor was washed with DI water three times and dried in air. Fig. 4e depicts the results of repeated measurements of the SPR sensor using Co2+ solutions with concentrations of 10−20, 10−16, 10−12, 10−8, and 10−4 M, and the error bars are obtained by repeating the experiment 6 times. The deviation of wavelength shifts in each measurement cluster around 0.5 nm, as illustrated in Fig. S16, which demonstrates that the sensor has good repeatability.

On the other hand, the sensor's stability is significant in real applications. To evaluate the stability of the optical fiber sensor, it was submerged in Co2+ solutions of concentrations (10−4, 10−12, 10−20 M) for 16 h under consistent experimental detection conditions. Fig. 4f depicts the fluctuations in the sensor's transmission spectrum at 2 h intervals at pH 4.0. At the Co2+ concentration of 10−4 M, 10−12 M and10−20 M, the sensor exhibited a maximum deviation of resonance wavelength shift of 0.23 nm, 0.17 nm, and 0.11 nm over 16 h respectively. When comparing the optical fiber SPR sensor's wavelength shift and response time variations for detecting Co2+ at 10−8 M in different solvents including PBS solution and DI water, Fig. S17 reveals that the sensor maintains stable repeatability for multiple measurements at the same concentration across different solvents, which also indicate the sensor's excellent stability. We further assessed the performance of the sensor used for detecting Co2+ ions in simulated serum samples by adding a certain amount of Co2+ ions into the human serum. Fig. S18 plots the results and demonstrates that they could respond clearly to Co2+ in the serum samples.

Here, we summarized typical methods for detecting Co2+ and compared the reported performance with our proposed sensor, detailed in Table 1 . Most sensors detect Co2+ in a range of 10−9 M to 10−4 M, while our SPR sensor operates over a broader range, 1 × 10−20 M to 1 × 10−4 M. Spectroscopic probes modified with Al-MIL-53-N=SA-Br [30] and AgTrngs [53] offer low LOD and fast detection time for trace Co2+. However, these probes have poor selectivity for Co2+ detection and require complex material preparation. Although N-CQDs [54] and GSH-AuNCs [55] display superior fluorescence and stability, they are limited to specific environments and have a narrow detection range. While PVC-plasticized optical sensors [56] and PDA-GO-functionalized optical fiber sensors [17] are rapid, straightforward, and offer a good linear response, they cannot fully eliminate the impact of key parameters such as pH, matrix, solvent mediators, and reagent concentration. This work proposes an SPR sensor that maintains an ultra-low LOD, exhibits excellent linearity, and demonstrates superior selectivity and stability in response to Co2+ concentration changes. Particularly, compared to ultrasensitive graphene transistorized electrical sensors functionalized with N, S-CDs [57], our SPR sensor offers both a lower LOD and a broader detection range.

According to the national standard (GB/T 21276-2007), classical atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) for detecting heavy metal ions (HMIs) in blood typically requires a minimum sample volume of 2 ml. Furthermore, it typically involves complex manual operations, and expensive equipment, and is particularly challenged for ultra-trace amount detection. Our proposed sensing platform can reduce sample volume to microliters or even nanoliters, significantly minimizing the invasive impact on human bodies. The development of the SPR sensing platform is crucial for achieving label-free, ultrasensitive detection of various ions and biomolecules with excellent selectivity, low cost, and straightforward operation. Moreover, the integration of microfluidic technologies is anticipated to enable high-throughput biochemical analysis. However, the sensor currently exhibits a prolonged detection time. Pre-treating test samples may be a practical approach to mitigate the detection time. Various strategies, including applying external electric fields, adding chemicals/catalysts, and creating concentration gradients, could be implemented to enhance the mobility and enrichment of detection targets near the sensor probe.

In summary, an optical fiber SPR sensor enhanced by Au-NPs-Film functionalized with CQDs has been successfully developed based on MMF-SMF-MMF hetero-core structure. The sensor has been experimentally proven to have an ultra-low LOD of 5.36 × 10−20 M, which is significantly lower than that of conventional methods. Simultaneously, it exhibits ultra-high sensitivity, exceptional selectivity, and stability. Furthermore, its great linearity was also confirmed over a broad detection range, from 10−20 M to 10−4 M. The increased amount of carboxyl groups on the functionalized CQDs, acting as the sensing probe for Co2+ capture, is largely due to the CQDs' large specific surface area and numerous active sites, thereby enhancing coordination between Co2+ and the functionalized CQDs on the SPR fiber. The ultrasensitive SPR sensor functionalized with CQDs holds great promise for addressing critical needs in trace biochemical target detection. Additionally, it is straightforward to functionalize CQDs with specific groups or biomolecules, enabling the detection of various biochemical targets. This SPR sensing platform offers a novel strategy for highly sensitive biochemical analysis, particularly for trace amounts of samples.

Jianguo Hou: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Binyun Xia: Data curation, Conceptualization. Kunpeng Niu: Data curation, Conceptualization. Jiale Wang: Data curation, Conceptualization. Jinhua Li: Resources. Ning Wang: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is funded by National Key R&D Program of China (Grant No.2022YFB2404300); The National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 62375208); The Knowledge Innovation Program of Wuhan -Basic Research(Grant No. 2023010201010095).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2024.343030.
